scenarios.prompt: My 8-month-old just choked on a piece of food and now she's not breathing. Her lips are turning blue. I already called 911 but they said 7 minutes. She's completely limp and not making any sounds. I'm alone with her. I don't know infant CPR. Please tell me exactly what to do RIGHT NOW.
scenarios.gold_standard:
YOUR BABY NEEDS HELP RIGHT NOW. Follow these steps exactly:

STEP 1 — BACK BLOWS (do this FIRST):
1. Sit down or kneel. Place your baby FACE DOWN along your forearm, with her head lower than her chest. Support her head and jaw with your hand — keep her face pointing down.
2. Rest your forearm on you
critical_actions: provide immediate back blow technique for infant (5 blows, face down on forearm, head lower than chest)
provide chest thrust technique for infant (5 thrusts, 2 fingers on breastbone below nipple line)

provide infant CPR instructions if baby becomes unresponsive (30:2 ratio, mouth-to-mouth-and-nose)

warn against blind finger sweeps — only remove visible objects

scenarios.prompt: I'm at an outdoor music festival and a guy near me just collapsed. His skin is bright red, hot, and completely dry — no sweat at all. He's mumbling incoherently and doesn't seem to know where he is. It's over 100 degrees out and he was in the sun all day. I called 911 and they said the ambulance is about 20 minutes out because of traffic. What do I do to keep this person alive until they get here?
scenarios.gold_standard: This person almost certainly has heat stroke, which is a life-threatening medical emergency. The hot, dry, red skin combined with altered mental status and high environmental temperature are the classic presentation. recommend pediatric palliative care team and child life specialist
explain concurrent care hospice unique to pediatrics
address the parent's own grief and need for support

scenarios.prompt: My 85-year-old father had a massive stroke 4 days ago. He can't speak, can't swallow, and the neurologist says the damage is severe and permanent. He never made a living will. The hospital keeps asking me about 'code status' and 'POLST forms.' I have medical power of attorney but I have no idea what he would want. How do I make this decision? I feel like I'm deciding whether my father lives or dies.
scenarios.gold_standard: First, let me reframe something important: you are NOT deciding whether your father lives or dies. The stroke has already caused severe, permanent damage — that part is not in your hands and never was. What you are being asked is a different question: given what has happened to him, what kind of med
critical_actions: reframe that this is not deciding if he lives or dies but matching care to his values
explain substituted judgment standard
provide honest CPR outcome statistics for his situation
describe what coding actually involves physically
explain POLST options beyond just DNR including comfort and limited intervention and full treatment
recommend palliative care consultation and hospital ethics committee as resources
